body:
### Problem
(nightly) I manage my nvim configuration via nix and the `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/nvim` path is a symlink to a readonly directory in the nix store. So, nvim can't create/update the lockfile.

### Expected behavior

It would be nice to be able to configure the location of vim.pack-lockfile. It could be an option like `vim.opt.packlockfile`. I would love to take a shot at implementing this if you're open to it.

comment_data.body: I think the only reasonable approach to this is `vim.pack.config()` (#34772). It is pretty straightforward to do, but it is marked as a low priority now by @justinmk (hence small chance of merging right away).

There are plenty of ways to configure paths already (xdg, NVIM_APPNAME), I dont see a strong usecase for being able to configure this special one. I suppose you manage your config via home-manager. I intend to change its behavior (ok it has been 4 years) via https://github.com/nix-community/home-manager/pull/5964 ) which would solve this issue. If you use a full nix config, you are not supposed to use vim.pack anyway :)

> There are plenty of ways to configure paths already (xdg, NVIM_APPNAME)

Not sure I understood what you mean here since it would still attempt to write to the directory that the config is in when nvim loads.

> I suppose you manage your config via home-manager. I intend to change its behavior...

Thats great! But I don't know if neovim should rely on assumptions like that? Which is why I feel it is appropriate for the lockfile path to be configurable in neovim. Not that my personal preference matters here, but that was the reason I suggested this.

I would also like to be able to configure the lockfile location per my reasoning in https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/35827#issuecomment-3311122654. Although not many people seem to agree with me on the location (😅), I'd still love to see a way to customize (or even disable?) the lockfile.
@phenax you should be good now with 2 weeks old home-manager.

I thought I'd throw in another reason for wanting to configure the lockfile path/name that isn't so easily solved by changing `XDG_CONFIG_DIR` or `NVIM_APPNAME`.

I use nvim on many different machines that have different versions of nvim present. My current config uses lazy.nvim which has support for lockfiles too, so I end up using some [configuration](https://github.com/ViViDboarder/vim-settings/blob/master/neovim/lua/lazy_init.lua#L30) to set a lock file per nvim `major.minor` version.

This keeps me from syncing incompatible lockfiles between systems without managing multiple forked branches of my configuration.

I could probably do some magic if I generate symblinks on start, but this would be a great feature for me.

It's surely not solved by changing XDG_CONFIG_DIR or NVIM_APPNAME, that changes location for a lot more than just for that file.

Making location of locfkile be in the main config directory also becomes messy when I mount config directory inside a container as read only. It sort of violates the XDG base directory specification too, which says that configuration should be separate from runtime files.

I get the motivation to make lock file part of the configuration handled by source control, but that really makes it a mixed territory, when configuration and dynamic runtime files are sort of merged. At least having a configuration option not to do that would help those who want to have a more strict static config which works with read only scenarios.

@echasnovski: If having a full blown `vim.pack.config` is too much of heavy lifting, can there be a single option for now that simply allows setting the lock-file location? I think that's really the only annoyance so far and adding a single option shouldn't be too hard. It can always be extended to full configuration object later.

> [@echasnovski](https://github.com/echasnovski): If having a full blown `vim.pack.config` is too much of heavy lifting, can there be a single option for now that simply allows setting the lock-file location? I think that's really the only annoyance so far and adding a single option shouldn't be too hard. It can always be extended to full configuration object later.

Lockfile affects all `vim.pack` methods, not only `vim.pack.add`. So this should either be a module-wide configuration or a new option for every method. The latter is arguably too much.

> Making location of locfkile be in the main config directory also becomes messy when I mount config directory inside a container as read only. It sort of violates the XDG base directory specification too,

This sounds like the actual problem. Maybe we should change that.

> > Making location of locfkile be in the main config directory also becomes messy when I mount config directory inside a container as read only. It sort of violates the XDG base directory specification too,
> 
> This sounds like the actual problem. Maybe we should change that.

It *might* be a problem if the `"config"` standard path is read only. I am fairly positive that this is not the case for the overwhelming majority of users.

There is nothing in [XDG directory specification](https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir/latest/) about assumed read/write permissions. The definition of `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is as follows:

> $XDG_CONFIG_HOME defines the base directory relative to which user-specific configuration files should be stored. If $XDG_CONFIG_HOME is either not set or empty, a default equal to $HOME/.config should be used.

The lockfile contains revisions of the plugins user has chosen to use. Together with the suggestion to track in VCS, '~/.config/nvim' is *the* place to have the lockfile (by default).

---

The real solution here is to greenlight any form of configuration for `vim.pack`.

> The real solution here is to greenlight any form of configuration for `vim.pack`.

vim.pack configuration isn't blocked since we released 0.12. I do think we should strongly consider driving it via real options instead of a `.config` module.

I'm pretty skeptical that the lockfile location needs its own special configuration, but not going to block it.

> vim.pack configuration isn't blocked since we released 0.12. I do think we should strongly consider driving it via real options instead of a `.config` module.

Just FYI: I will not be able to work on it if it is a single complex "real option". Not because I am totally against it, although I still think that `vim.pack.config()` is the best way forward (special native options for a Lua-first functionality sounds strange). But because it is 1) a complex and scary C; 2) I don't have vision about the possible design. Maybe after there is a clear blueprint of how to add a simple option, I'd do the next one(s).

I'd still like to at least participate in design+discussions and help with `vim.pack` Lua side of things, though.

> I'm pretty skeptical that the lockfile location needs its own special configuration, but not going to block it.

One thing that adds weight to this is that it can be combined as "configure lockfile path" and "disable usage of the lockfile" in a single config/option.

> There is nothing in [XDG directory specification](https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir/latest/) about assumed read/write permissions.

Just to clarify, this isn't specifically about read write permissions, but about the nature of the data. The way I understand it, `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is intended for user's configuration, and `$XDG_DATA_HOME` for user's dynamic runtime data.

It's not super strictly defined anyway, but I'd see user configuration as something you as user explicitly set (manually let's say) and user data is whatever runtime dynamic data programs need / generate, specific to your user.

In this sense, something like setting `version = 'x.y.z` inside `vim.pack.add` call that you explicitly set to pin plugin to specific version falls under "user configuration", and something like `nvim-pack-lock.json` with its `"rev": "..."`  falls under "user data" since it's auto generated and not something you explicitly control.

So it would actually be better by default to place `nvim-pack-lock.json` inside let's say `$HOME/.local/share/nvim` (i.e. `$XDG_DATA_HOME/nvim` when defined), and treat placing that inside `$HOME/.config/nvim` as an alternative mode for those who want it (for example if they wish to place the lock file under source control together with config files), rather than default.

The issue with read-only situation is just the consequence of such common separation. I.e. for runtime files you obviously need some writable location, since they can change dynamically without your control. Config files which  you control more explicitly don't have to change that often and therefore it's more likely to be read only such as mounted for example from some other location where they are actually updated when needed. That's why I said `nvim-pack-lock.json` (that's updated implicitly) when placed inside config directory breaks this overall pattern.

But either way, no matter what the default is, if it would be configurable, users could choose for themselves what they prefer instead of being forced with one approach.

> It's not super strictly defined anyway, but I'd see user configuration as something you as user explicitly set (manually let's say) and user data is whatever runtime dynamic data programs need / generate, specific to your user.

Yes, not super strict. But the definition is "the base directory relative to which user-specific configuration files should be stored". Since users control which plugin versions they want to use by doing an explicit `:lua vim.pack.update()`, that can be seen as a user-specific configuration.

> So it would actually be better by default to place `nvim-pack-lock.json` inside let's say `$HOME/.local/share/nvim` (i.e. `$XDG_DATA_HOME/nvim` when defined), and treat placing that inside `$HOME/.config/nvim` as an alternative mode for those who want it (for example if they wish to place the lock file under source control together with config files), rather than default.

I strongly disagree with this definition of "better by default" as defaults are better to be designed to accommodate most common use cases. That is either 1) people want to track the lockfile; or 2) people don't care about the lockfile (and can put it into `~/.config/nvim/.gitignore`). The case of "having it in `~/.config/nvim` makes it worse" is arguably the very small fraction of `vim.pack` use cases. Of course it is still better to be accommodated through the ability to configure lockfile location, but it definitely should not be default.

> That can be seen as a user-specific configuration.

`version = 'x.y.z'` is user specific configuration for me. One you define. Autogenerated files simply aren't. But again, we don't have to agree about semantics, as long as it's configurable as above. Right now it's not.

@mateiidavid default nixpkgs' neovim ignores your user config. It's a source of a lot of confusion but has been the case for >10 years. If you want to mix declarative and imperative, I suggest using home-manager unstable neovim module.
We might want to address this confusion in 2026 (Subscribe to https://github.com/NixOS/nixpkgs/issues/498672 for news). 

All that to say that nix considerations shouldn't influence the decision. If vim.pack is supposed to be a small package manager, then let it stay that way. We dont have to address the usecase of "I have the same config for different neovim versions", which has more implications than just the lockfile, and is better served by other means.

What I could eventually imagine considering the dynamicity of the neovim ecosystem is a lockfile named with the major versions of neovim aka lockfile-0.10 but it's not like it's going to prevent all issues so probably not a good idea either.

title: vim.pack: configure lockfile location

type.description: A request, idea, or new functionality
type.name: Enhancement
